collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2024/2025. A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2409.14507 (NeurIPS 2025 Oral)
collisions.overlap: Defines feature absorption, the probe-based absorption metric, and demonstrates absorption on the first-letter spelling task using Gemma 2 2B and Gemma Scope SAEs. The proposal extends this metric to five new domains and adds confound decomposition.

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al., 2025. SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2503.09532 (ICML 2025)
collisions.overlap: SAEBench includes absorption metric (first-letter only) and RAVEL benchmark testing city-country, city-continent, city-language disentanglement on Gemma 2 2B. RAVEL measures disentanglement (causal intervention accuracy), NOT absorption rates (probe-based false negative detection). Different metrics measuring different failure modes.

collisions.paper: Peter & Sogaard, 2025. Empirical Evaluation of Progressive Coding for Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2505.00190
collisions.overlap: Connects SAEs to progressive/successive coding and rate-distortion trade-offs. Mentions Equitz & Cover (1991) as conceptual background. However, does NOT study absorption, does NOT define CMI = I(X; f_parent | f_child), does NOT derive absorption optimality conditions, and does NOT predict per-feature absorption. Asks 'how to train efficient progressive SAEs' vs. our 'when is absorption necessary.'

collisions.paper: Chanin, Dulka & Garriga-Alonso, 2025. Feature Hedging: Correlated Features Break Narrow Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2505.11756
collisions.overlap: Decomposes SAE failures into hedging vs. absorption (two components). The proposal extends to three components (hierarchy-driven, hedging, reconstruction error) with multi-L0 profiling and threshold sensitivity analysis.

collisions.paper: Luo et al., 2026. From Atoms to Trees: Building a Structured Feature Forest with Hierarchical Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2602.11881
collisions.overlap: Proposes Hierarchical SAE that reduces absorption on SAEBench first-letter benchmark. Does NOT measure per-domain absorption rates, perform confound decomposition, or apply rate-distortion analysis. Architectural solution vs. our diagnostic characterization.

collisions.paper: Costa et al., 2025. From Flat to Hierarchical: Extracting Sparse Representations with Matching Pursuit. arXiv:2506.03093 (NeurIPS 2025 Poster)
collisions.overlap: Shows hierarchical concepts induce conditionally orthogonal features. Proposes MP-SAE architecture. Relevant to theoretical grounding but NOT absorption detection or rate-distortion diagnostics.

collisions.paper: Korznikov et al., 2025. OrtSAE: Orthogonal Sparse Autoencoders Uncover Atomic Features. arXiv:2509.22033 (ICLR 2026)
collisions.overlap: Reduces absorption by 65% via orthogonality constraints. Evaluates on SAEBench first-letter benchmark. Architectural solution; does NOT characterize absorption cross-domain or provide information-theoretic diagnostics.

collisions.paper: Bussmann et al., 2025. Learning Multi-Level Features with Matryoshka Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2503.17547
collisions.overlap: Matryoshka SAEs reduce absorption via nested training. Best-performing architecture on absorption metric. Relevant as baseline comparison.

collisions.paper: Tang et al., 2025. A Unified Theory of Sparse Dictionary Learning in Mechanistic Interpretability. arXiv:2512.05534
collisions.overlap: Unified theoretical framework proving global minima correspond to correct recovery, spurious local minima exhibit absorption. Proposes feature anchoring. Different theoretical angle (optimization landscape vs. information theory).

collisions.paper: Cui et al., 2025/2026. On the Limits of Sparse Autoencoders: A Theoretical Framework and Reweighted Remedy. arXiv:2506.15963
collisions.overlap: Impossibility results for full feature recovery. Different from the proposal's question of when absorption is information-theoretically necessary.

collisions.paper: Bereska et al., 2025. Superposition as Lossy Compression: Measure with SAEs. arXiv:2512.13568
collisions.overlap: Information-theoretic framework using Shannon entropy on SAE activations to measure superposition. Different quantity (superposition degree vs. absorption diagnostic) and different method (entropy vs. CMI).

collisions.paper: O'Neill et al., 2025. Resurrecting the Salmon: Domain-Specific SAEs. arXiv:2508.09363
collisions.overlap: Domain-specific SAEs reduce absorption. Does NOT measure absorption rates per domain using Chanin metric.

collisions.paper: DeepMind Safety Team, 2025. Negative Results for SAEs on Downstream Tasks. Medium blog post
collisions.overlap: SAE probes underperform dense probes on safety tasks. Consistent with absorption but does NOT measure absorption directly.

collisions.paper: LessWrong post: Looking for feature absorption automatically. Author anonymous.
collisions.overlap: Failed unsupervised absorption detection attempt. The proposal addresses this negative result with refined methods.

collisions.paper: Song et al., 2025. Position: Mechanistic Interpretability Should Prioritize Feature Consistency in SAEs. arXiv:2505.20254
collisions.overlap: Shows SAE features inconsistent across training runs. General concern affecting all SAE-based research, not specifically absorption.

collisions.paper: KronSAE, 2025. Kronecker Factorization Improves Efficiency and Interpretability of SAEs. arXiv:2505.22255
collisions.overlap: Reduces absorption via Kronecker decomposition. Architectural solution, not diagnostic characterization.

collisions.paper: Tian et al., 2025. Measuring Sparse Autoencoder Feature Sensitivity. arXiv:2509.23717 (NeurIPS 2025 MI Workshop Spotlight)
collisions.overlap: Evaluates feature reliability via generation-based testing. Tangentially related to absorption (feature failure to activate) but different method and question.

candidates.differentiation_notes: The core novelty is defensible across three axes despite a more competitive landscape: (1) No prior work measures absorption rates across multiple knowledge hierarchy domains (city-country, city-continent, city-language, animal-class) using the Chanin metric on Gemma 2 2B. SAEBench RAVEL tests disentanglement, not absorption rates. HSAE and OrtSAE evaluate absorption only on the first-letter SAEBench benchmark. (2) The CMI = I(X; f_parent | f_child) diagnostic from the successive refinement theorem is entirely novel. Peter & Sogaard (2025) connect SAEs to progressive coding/rate-distortion but do NOT address absorption or define CMI-based diagnostics. Bereska et al. (2025) use Shannon entropy for superposition, a different quantity entirely. (3) The three-component confound decomposition with multi-L0 profiling extends beyond Chanin et al.'s (2025) two-component hedging-absorption decomposition. Critical differentiations: (a) from Peter & Sogaard -- their question is 'efficient training' vs. our 'diagnostic for absorption necessity'; (b) from RAVEL -- disentanglement vs. absorption rates; (c) from hedging paper -- two components vs. three + multi-L0 + threshold sensitivity.

collisions.paper: Chanin, Dulka & Garriga-Alonso, 2025. Feature Hedging. arXiv:2505.11756
collisions.overlap: Already decomposes SAE failures into hedging vs. absorption. Proposes balanced Matryoshka SAE.

collisions.paper: Chanin & Garriga-Alonso, 2025. Sparse but Wrong: Incorrect L0 Leads to Incorrect Features.
collisions.overlap: Shows incorrect L0 causes feature distortion. Covers the L0 confound component.

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al., 2025. SAEBench. arXiv:2503.09532
collisions.overlap: Threshold sensitivity across L0 ranges partially covered.

candidates.differentiation_notes: The hedging paper (Chanin et al., 2025) significantly reduces novelty. The immune escape test (zeroing child to see parent recovery) and three-component decomposition remain novel. Only pursue if front-runner fails.

collisions.paper: Peter & Sogaard, 2025. Progressive Coding for SAEs. arXiv:2505.00190
collisions.overlap: Connects SAEs to progressive/successive coding. Does NOT formalize absorption optimality via CMI or derive diagnostic conditions. Weak overlap: shared conceptual background (Equitz & Cover), completely different application.

collisions.paper: Bereska et al., 2025. Superposition as Lossy Compression. arXiv:2512.13568
collisions.overlap: Information-theoretic SAE analysis. Different quantity (superposition via entropy vs. absorption via CMI).

collisions.paper: Bussmann et al., 2025. Matryoshka SAEs. arXiv:2503.17547
collisions.overlap: Implicitly implements progressive coding but does NOT formalize via successive refinement theorem.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Retains highest per-claim novelty. The CMI threshold for absorption optimality, the Markov chain condition for lossless absorption, and the lateral inhibition bifurcation analysis remain entirely novel. Peter & Sogaard (2025) creates intellectual context (rate-distortion is relevant to SAEs) without overlapping on the diagnostic application. This is a pivot target if cross-domain empirics fail.

dropped.reason: Theoretical landscape saturated by Cui et al. (2506.15963), Tang et al. (2512.05534), and arXiv:2506.14002. Correctly dropped in synthesis round 2.
Superseded by cand_cross_domain_ratedistortion. Unsupervised detection de-prioritized after pilot ITAC evidence. Rate-distortion elevated to co-primary.

search_metadata.update_reason: Updated novelty check for revised front-runner (cand_cross_domain_ratedistortion) with rate-distortion elevated to co-primary and unsupervised detection de-prioritized
new_papers_since_last_check: Peter & Sogaard, 2025. Progressive Coding. arXiv:2505.00190 (closest new overlap with rate-distortion framing)
Luo et al., 2026. From Atoms to Trees (HSAE). arXiv:2602.11881 (hierarchical SAE, absorption evaluation)
Korznikov et al., 2025. OrtSAE. arXiv:2509.22033 (orthogonal SAE, reduces absorption 65%)
KronSAE, 2025. arXiv:2505.22255 (Kronecker factorized SAE, reduces absorption)
Tian et al., 2025. Feature Sensitivity. arXiv:2509.23717 (feature reliability measurement)
Song et al., 2025. Feature Consistency. arXiv:2505.20254 (SAE reproducibility)
O'Neill et al., 2025. Resurrecting the Salmon. arXiv:2508.09363 (domain-specific SAEs)
key_differentiations_required: Peter & Sogaard (progressive coding) vs. our rate-distortion diagnostic -- engineering vs. diagnostic question
RAVEL (disentanglement) vs. our absorption rates -- different metrics, different failure modes
Feature Hedging (two-component) vs. our confound decomposition (three-component + multi-L0)
